mappings.rationaleAI: The second law of thermodynamics covering Carnot, Clausius, Kelvin, entropy, and Boltzmann's statistical interpretation directly addresses A.3 (the second law) and A.9 (statistical thermodynamics).

The history of heat from caloric theory to kinetic theory, Joule's mechanical equivalent of heat, and the first and second laws of thermodynamics address A.2 (first law), A.3 (second law), and the definition and theories of heat (A.4.c).

Kinetic theory explaining macroscopic properties through molecular motion, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, and the ideal gas law from molecular collisions address theories of heat (A.4.c.ii), statistical thermodynamics (A.9), and the particle-description approach to gases (B.3).

Water's liquid-state properties, hydrogen bonding, surface tension, capillary action, universal solvent capacity, and phase transitions address the liquid state (C), solutions and solubility (D), and surface tension (E.1).

Absolute zero, the third law of thermodynamics, and quantum phenomena at ultra-low temperatures (superconductivity, superfluidity, Bose-Einstein condensation) address A.6 (third law) and properties of matter at low temperatures (G.1).

States of matter covering phase transitions, critical points, supercritical fluids, plasmas, and matter under extreme conditions comprehensively address phase changes (A.5.c), gaseous (B), liquid (C), plasma (F), and extreme condition states (G).
Plasma physics covering ionisation, Debye shielding, collective behaviour, tokamaks, and prevalence in nature (stars, solar wind, lightning) comprehensively addresses the plasma state (F).

Superconductivity covering the 1911 discovery, BCS theory, Cooper pairs, the Meissner effect, and high-temperature superconductors directly addresses special physical phenomena at very low temperatures (G.1.b).
Perpetual motion machines and their thermodynamic impossibility established by the first and second laws directly illustrate the first law (A.2) and second law (A.3) of thermodynamics.

Glass as an amorphous solid with debates about its liquid-solid classification, the glass transition temperature, and its anomalous properties address molecular structure of liquids (C.2) and properties of liquids (C.3).
